title: Whittled ash dibber
subtitle: A pointed green-wood dibber for seed sowing and plug planting
excerpt: A dibber; the pointed stick used to make seed holes in soil; takes 30 minutes to whittle from a green ash rod and lasts for years. This version is sized for plug-plant transplanting: 30 cm long, tapered to a blunt point at one end, with a simple grip knob at the other.
sourceNotes: Traditional British kitchen-garden toolmaking; Cassell's Household Guide (1880) vol. I for the pointed-stick dibber form.

content.text: A dibber is a pointed stick and nothing more: push it into the soil to make a hole, drop in a seed or plug, firm the soil back. A good dibber is 30 cm long, as thick as a thumb at the grip end, and tapered to a blunt point over the bottom 8 cm. This one starts from a fresh ash rod;
green wood
carves quickly and the dibber does not need to be food-safe or indoor-finished. A hazel rod works identically; willow is softer and less durable; oak is heavier but lasts longer in wet soil.
The wood

A freshly cut ash rod: 33 cm long, 2.5 to 3 cm in diameter. Coppice material is ideal; ash from a coppiced stool will be straight-grained. Cut the rod in spring or summer when the bark is easy to remove. Hazel is the traditional dibber wood in British kitchen gardens and is equally good.
Tools
A sloyd knife and a leather strop. No other tools needed. The sloyd knife should be sharp enough to peel bark cleanly in one pass; test on a twig before starting the dibber.
Method
Use
push cuts
throughout. The rod is round and rolls under the hand; grip it firmly against the knee or workbench rather than holding it freely in the air. Strop after every 10 minutes of work.

Peel the bark. Hold the rod against the inside of the knee, sloyd knife at a low angle, and push the blade along the rod from one end to the other. The bark peels in a single pass if the wood is green and the knife sharp. Repeat around the circumference until the rod is clean. Ash bark pulls free easily in spring; in autumn it needs the knife to score it first.

Shape the point at the lower end over the bottom 8 cm: a consistent 8-faceted taper down to a blunt point. Push cuts along each face of the taper, working from the tip toward the middle of the rod. The taper should be even on all sides; hold the rod up and rotate it to check. The point is blunt, not sharp: a sharp point will break in stony soil.

Cut a simple grip knob at the upper end: remove a 2 cm ring of wood 3 cm from the top, leaving a raised collar that stops the dibber slipping through the grip. Three stop cuts; one at each edge of the 2 cm ring; followed by push cuts to remove the wood between them.
Leave the
blank
to dry for 2 weeks before first use. A green dibber in wet soil can soften and lose its point; a partially dried one resists soil moisture much better.
Finishing
Leave unfinished or apply a coat of boiled linseed oil to the point and lower 10 cm. The oil keeps soil from opening the grain. Re-oil annually. The rest of the dibber needs no treatment.
Variations
Hazel: lighter, slightly less straight grain. The traditional dibber material in UK kitchen gardens.
Short seed dibber: 15 cm, 1.5 cm diameter, tapered to 5 cm. For fine seed sowing in module trays.
Care
Store dry between uses. Rinse off soil after use and leave to dry before putting away. Re-oil the point each spring.

attrs.intro: Common problems with the whittled dibber.

items.fix: Keep the point blunt; a 10 mm diameter at the tip is the minimum. Dry the dibber for a full 2 weeks before use.
items.cause: Point too sharp or wood not yet fully dried

items.fix: Score the bark with the knife tip before peeling. Strop the knife before starting.
items.cause: Wood not fully green, or knife too dull
items.symptom: Bark tears in strips rather than peeling cleanly

items.fix: Mark the 8 taper faces in pencil before starting. Remove the same number of cuts from each face.
items.cause: Cuts not starting at the same point on each face
items.symptom: Taper uneven; thicker on one side

attrs.heading: What can go wrong
